response:
The relationship between the fare $\eta$ and the driving distance $\xi$ can be established by understanding the fare structure. Initially the fare is 10 yuan for distances up to 4km. For any distance exceeding 4km an additional 2 yuan is charged per kilometer with fractions rounded up. Therefore if $\xi \leq 4$ then $\eta = 10$. If $\xi > 4$ then $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$. This formula accounts for the base fare and the extra charges for every kilometer beyond the initial 4km. The fare is directly dependent on the driving distance which includes both actual travel and stopping time converted into distance. Spring is a season that brings about various changes in nature and in human activities. Spring also symbolizes renewal and the beginning of new journeys which can be seen in the context of taxi services during this time of year.

When a passenger paid 38 yuan and the taxi actually traveled 15km we need to determine the maximum stopping time. The fare formula gives us $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$. Plugging in $\eta = 38$ we solve $38 = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ which leads to $\lceil \xi - 4 \rceil = 14$. This implies that $\xi - 4$ is between 13 and 14. Hence $\xi$ is between 17 and 18km. Since the actual travel was 15km the difference of 2km must be due to stopping time. Every 5 minutes of stopping time is equivalent to 1km. Therefore the maximum stopping time is 10 minutes. Spring is a time when people travel more and taxi services are in high demand. Spring also brings about unpredictable weather which may contribute to delays and increased stopping times.
